_templates: Three scenario templates — pick the one matching your agent topology.

TEMPLATE_1_SINGLE_SOCIAL_AGENT._comment: One social conversation agent talking directly to the user. No back-end agents. No session_config needed.
TEMPLATE_1_SINGLE_SOCIAL_AGENT.world_state_seed: Prose description of the world state at the start of this scenario. Third person. Factual. Sets the shared context all agents inhabit.

agents._comment: Loads agents/sara.json. agent_type is read from the agent file.

TEMPLATE_1_SINGLE_SOCIAL_AGENT._initial_queue_note: initial_queue is not yet wired — AgentQueue initialises from persisted state. Reserved for future use.
_omitted: session_config — not needed for single-agent scenarios

TEMPLATE_2_FORMAL_PLUS_TASK_AGENT._comment: A user-facing formal/relay agent backed by one task-decomposition agent. session_config is required to name relay and task agent IDs explicitly, because both the relay role (front-end) and task role (back-end) cannot be inferred from user_facing alone when a back-end relay agent is also present.
TEMPLATE_2_FORMAL_PLUS_TASK_AGENT.world_state_seed: Prose description of the world context for this task scenario.

session_config._comment: Explicit role naming for _post_turn_enqueue routing. relay_agent_id is the agent that relays signals to/from the user. task_agent_id is the primary back-end agent. Only needed when user_facing alone is ambiguous.

TEMPLATE_2_FORMAL_PLUS_TASK_AGENT._initial_queue_note: initial_queue is not yet wired — reserved for future use.

TEMPLATE_3_MULTI_AGENT_SOCIAL._comment: Multiple social agents sharing a conversation. All are user-facing. No back-end task agents. No session_config needed — user_facing drives routing.
TEMPLATE_3_MULTI_AGENT_SOCIAL.world_state_seed: Prose description of the shared world context all agents inhabit.

agents._comment: First agent to speak by default (first in list sets _user_facing_agent).
Second agent. Called explicitly via next_speaker='derek' in run_pipeline.

TEMPLATE_3_MULTI_AGENT_SOCIAL._routing_note: In multi-social scenarios, pass next_speaker=<agent_id> to run_pipeline to control turn order. The first user_facing agent in the list is the default respondent.
_omitted: session_config — not needed when all agents are user-facing

id.notes: Unique identifier. Used for logging and observability.

medium.notes: Describes the communication channel. Injected into prompts. Examples: 'online text chat', 'voice call', 'in-person conversation'.

world_state_seed.notes: Prose describing the world at session start. Third person. Seeded once on first load; the Worldview system evolves it from there. Omit if world state is not relevant to this scenario.

agents.notes: Roster of all agents in this scenario. Only written on first load — subsequent turns restore from persisted state. If omitted, caller must pass session_agents= to run_pipeline explicitly.

entry_fields.agent_id: string, required — must match the stem of an agents/<id>.json file
entry_fields.user_facing: bool, required — true if this agent participates in the user-facing conversation
entry_fields._comment: string, optional — ignored by the loader, for human readability only

session_config.notes: Only needed when _post_turn_enqueue cannot infer relay/task roles from user_facing alone. Typical case: a back-end relay agent sits between the user-facing agent and one or more task agents.

fields.relay_agent_id: string — agent that relays signals between back-end and user. Written to global:_relay_agent_id.
fields.task_agent_id: string — primary back-end task agent. Written to global:_task_agent_id.

initial_queue.status: NOT YET WIRED — reserved for future use
initial_queue.notes: Intended to seed the AgentQueue at session start so the first turn can be agent-initiated rather than user-initiated. Currently AgentQueue.from_state() initialises from persisted state only.
